channel.title: Politics on Reflections in the Wilderness of New Atlantis
channel.description: Recent content in Politics on Reflections in the Wilderness of New Atlantis

item.title: Do we worship God or does God watch us worship ourselves?
item.description:
Today my intention is to briefly describe the hierarchies as they are related to God and worship. To begin with, what is God? God is the absolute totality. This includes both the physical world and the “higher” world, which some may call ideal, or the spiritual. For the purposes of today, we shall call this “higher” world the spiritual. Often times, these two worlds are conceived of differently, as antinomies held apart, but connected by something higher. That conception is quite wrong, they are already unified. If they weren’t, the classically defined conception of God would make no sense, nor would its usage in written testimony. Thus, God is the ultimate source from which all being and differentiation emanates. The true “One God” of authentic monotheism points us here, but the human mind can only approach it through manifestations, including the contours of its logical necessity. Within this “oneness”, we may re-introduce spirit, or the spiritual world, as the animating, formative force by which God interacts with the world. Important to our understanding is the etymological foundation of the word. Spirit derives from the Latin spiritus, meaning “breath, breathing, life”, etc. This itself comes from spirare, “to breath”, which connects to the Greek pneuma(πνεῦμα), also meaning breath, the Greek psykhē (ψυχή), meaning breath, the Sanskrit ātman, also meaning breath. Worth noting is the Hebrew word ruach, literally meaning “wind”, but translated as “the spirit”, and various other words that draw this same allusion! So what’s the deal with the breath allusion? Well, it’s certainly not incidental or accidental in any way. Breath is the invisible force that animates the living being. It is the immediate experience of the vital principle entering from within, sustaining life from within, and then returning without. There is modern day word play here with the phrase “catching one’s breath”. The phrase suggests a possession where it is your breath, while “catching” it, is like catching the wind. It’s never truly yours, nor do you manufacture or command your breath. You receive or synchronize yourself with it.

Just an aside, “God” also has a funny etymology, being obscure and debated. Current theories connect it Proto-Indo-European roots *ǵʰutós (invoked), from *ǵʰew- (to call, to invoke), which suggests to one the title: “that which is invoked”. Unlike spirit, which describes a very experiential force, “God” is a title whose origin points to a ritual action and invocation, which implies a kind of alienation. When naming “God” without concretely knowing what we are naming, we don’t exactly know what we are invoking. This will subsequently be proved.

Spirit, on the other hand, is very concretely known by man as an activity that he himself comes into contact with and is constituted by. If not by the ideal principles of the physical body, then man comes to know spirit by the very breath of nature, spiritual beings whose exhalation appears to us as crystal formation, plant growth, and flowing stream of a river, providing us with an understanding of matter and physicality as the condensed “breath” of spirit. Of course, the crystal as an object is not a spirit, but the formative activity that produces the crystal is spiritual (named as an elemental). In this way, the physical world and the spiritual world have this sort of relationship.

We have, before us, an adequate definition of “spirit”. Spirit is self-generating activity. Likewise, its qualities, it’s intelligence, scope, and power, are delineated by the level from which it generates itself. Consider it a spectrum of creative power. God is this creative power, but there are qualitative distinctions between, say, the spirit flowing in a river, and the spirit in human genius. Both are definitely self-generating activities, but of vastly different orders. These different orders are called the hierarchies; they are the graduated, intelligent levels of this unitary power. Consequently, “Spirit” becomes concretely understood as a domain of hierarchies constituting the spiritual world. To speak of “spirit” is to speak of this totality as a structured continuum of creative beings. On the other hand, to deny the hierarchies is to deny the observable, graded complexity of creative activity in the universe, reducing it either to some vague monism (God), or blind materialism (which is also God, but this God is called “chance”).

Barring those latter two reductions, when people speak vaguely of “God”, they are usually unknowingly referring to one of the lower levels of this hierarchy. Rightfully, they grasp a self-generating activity in both the physical world and the spiritual, but they wrongfully attribute God to some lower order of power. Before us are a few various relevant hierarchical members of which they may refer: The “I”, or human ego, is the individual human spirit, developed since the last death (if you haven’t accepted reincarnation as a fact of life already, you might need another few incarnations to get caught up to speed). In modern discourse, it is often unconsciously deified. Angeloi, or angels, are beings of the hierarchy immediately above the human “I”. Each human has a specific angel as a guide and protector. This is the spiritual entity most accessible to the modern individual’s consciousness. When changing course in life abruptly and having this change result in avoiding this or that accident, you can thank your guardian angel. Archangeloi, or archangels, are beings of a higher hierarchy who govern the collective impulses, spirits (or breaths) of an epoch, and larger cultural streams. Relating them is beyond the scope of discussion. There are other spiritual beings which constitute the “hierarchies”, but the ones stated are the most relevant for now.

The main point to convey today is that true spiritual progress is not achieved by leaping to an abstract “One God”. It involves an intentional concrete ascension through the knowledge of and relationship with these hierarchical beings, starting with the recognition of one’s own “I”, and its guardian angel. Modern professed monotheism can be considered a form of spiritual egoism, where under the name “God”, we either worship our own reflected self (the “I”), or our personalized spiritual servant (the Angel). The Maya by which this is accomplished is the very vagueness of the word “God”. When spirituality becomes a search for “God”, defined solely as that which validates my inner subjectivity and affirms my chosen paths, or sensibilities, we have entered a realm of self-worship. It is the worship of the self reflected by itself. This egoistic religious confession manifests itself via two core misattributing. The first is the sanctification of personal intuition and intellectual judgment. The Individual’s subjectivity, that sense of “what feels true to me is elevated to infallibility. The second finds the self as the sovereign concern. God is recast as a divine servant of personal destiny. The spiritual world (usually one’s Guardian Angel) so reduced to “the help” is commanded to bless the subject’s plans and ensure comfort. Prayer here becomes a negotiation for personal success, and how divine something is, is measured by its alignment with the subject’s will and subjective sense of well-being.

Modern religious consciousness inherits these problems. By confusing the universal with the personal, everyone’s “God” is ultimately their own “I”, or their personal angel, and shared spiritual truth and unity become an impossibility. Community begins to fracture into the collective of sovereign “I’s”, each with their own “truths”, leading to a pluralism of solipsisms. Growth is swiftly evaded. After all, an egoistic god never disagrees. They never demand change, or pulls us beyond our comfort zone, whether physically, or spiritually. The concrete cosmos- that world of hierarchies, elemental intelligences, cosmic purposes, and divine plans is dissolved into a fiery mist. Finally, that self-generating activity of spirit meets with the sovereign “I” resulting in a world of endless unmediated divine wills, inevitably running into conflict. Such a worldview is one of impotent chaos.

The decline of religious institutions, and the co-opting of them by evangelical mega-churches and opportunists, is not a coincidence. They are, in a sense, necessary products of humanity’s current stage of development. The task of this age demands that we experience our “I” in its full isolated subjectivity. It urges us to grasp our own freedom and individuality as an imperative for the evolution of spirit. Our own inner authority must be called on and set straight, otherwise the contraction-into-self found in the attitudes of “my God = my sovereignty/feelings” or the mischaracterization of God as an abstract being will lead us into ruin.

The “I” is beginning to experience the depth of its own interiority. These modern attitudes are thus the unconscious spiritual expressions of our current stage of development. The experience of interiority by the “I” intuitively feels the divinity which generated it, and in its immaturity proclaims itself a mighty oak, rather than recognizing itself as a seedling. We must ask ourselves in our daily rituals and practices: what are we actually worshiping?

item.title: Epistle to Polygenism
item.description:
Intro

Human biodiversity confronts us with a problem. Among the myriad physical features of man such as skin color, muscle and fat distribution and composition, epicanthic folds, head and body shapes, hair, eye color, and even blood characteristics, it pushes one towards the verdict that we are in some way different. Yet, breeding within two “disparate” populations, producing fully fertile offspring is possible and we, more or less, are capable of communicating, interacting, and understanding the life of each other despite this unnamed difference. The problem we are confronted with is the imposition of difference, with the perceived ontological grounding of oneness. How can we be both one (a single species) and many (so visibly diverse)? An answer one often finds aligned with a paradoxically regressive strain of thinking is the conclusion of polygenism. Paradoxical since it seems to find a home among those who would ally with the bias of immediacy - such as those who call themselves Christians, or enlightenment thinkers that tend to biological reductionism. The answer they provide is accepting of the immediacy of appearance, as well as behavior, and reduces it to some immutable fact of biology, or in some cases denigrates the status of the spiritual by raising appearance and behavior to some discrete and exclusive spiritual condition. Either path is the aforementioned attitude in new skin, mentioned because of the elephant in the room: racism. More will be said on this later as it relates to racial distinctions. For now, only a footnote can be made on the racist, and often Faustian, motivations behind rejecting Out of Africa, as well as upholding the fixed character of “racial” distinctions.

One historical answer to the question of the one and many in man was polygenism, the view that different races have separate origins, whether created separately by God or evolved from different apes/hominids. The idea is quite old. For example, the Roman Emperor Julian argued that the Germani and Scythians were too different from the Ethiopians to share common ancestry. Oral traditions such as the Bambuti creation myth told of three races made from three different kinds of Clay. Biblical polygenism regularly draws on the three sons of Noah as stand in for the “races”, or stock, of Black, White, and Yellow. In the 19th century, polygenism became a scientific movement, drawing Enlightenment rationalism and empiricism into it’s supposed methodology. People like Samuel George Morton and Josiah Clark Nott claimed that cranial measurements proved races were separate species. Polygenism persisted, persisted even after Darwin’s Origin of Species, with Carl Vogt and others arguing that different races descended from different apes. These days, modern science has decisively rejected polygenism. The Out of Africa genetic evidence shows that all living humans trace to a common African population within the last 200-300 thousand years. There is no genetic or fossil support for separate origins. Polygenism seems to have failed because it mistook the superficial adaptations for a more prior difference.

As it appears, the rejection of polygenism does not dissolve the phenomenon of diversity. We are one species, one blood (especially after the time of Jesus), one recent origin. And yet we appear in such a variety. My own explorations of this topic takes the unity and diversity seriously as the starting point of a spiritual investigation. Polygenism as an Enlightenment project and mere stipulation was an enterprise dead on arrival, for mainly the same reasons that taxonomy fails at being a science. Taxonomy, which as much as it can list difference in painstaking detail, cannot arrive at the generative process by which the differences in the elements it categorizes come about. Likewise, it cannot supply us with what stands as a valid separation or not. Yet, it was not wrong to sense that the diversity we see has deep roots. What polygenism lacked was a vision of how unity could give rise to multiplicity without fracturing into separate species forever. This is where spiritual science may offer us a missing link in the fact that the diversity we observe is a result of a gradual hardening and differentiation from an original spiritual unity. The physical form we now call Homo sapiens is the ultimate consolidation of a long process that began when human souls first descended into Earthly bodies in the Lemurian epoch, passed through the more plastic, liquid forms of the Atlantean epoch, and only fully solidified in the Post-Atlantean - the very period that Out of Africa genetic studies takes as it’s given object.

Why has Out of Africa become the consensus view of modern anthropology? The reason is that the evidence for a recent African origin of Homo sapiens is now so extensive that alternative models, while interesting, have now been set as aside. This is the conclusion that I have ultimately come to. There are a few alternative theories to consider before returning to Out of Africa. The first is the Out of Australia theory. This hypothesis suggested that anatomically modern humans first emerged in Australia, based on the claims of very early archaeological sites like Lake Mungo, as well as the suggestion that some ancient Australian remains showed unique morphological features. Proponents of this theory argued that the long temporal depth of human occupation indicates a possible origin. The problem is that all living humans, including Indigenous Australians, carry the same genetic markers that trace back to Africa. The oldest Y-chromosome and mitochondrial DNA lineages are found in Africa, not Australia. Moreover, the oldest fossils of Homo sapiens are African, far predating any Australian remains. Early Australian sites indicate an early migration of modern humans out of Africa, as opposed to an independent origin. Hominin Network Theory, in recent years, proposes that the fact that Neanderthals and Denisovans interbred with modern humans, creating essentially a tangled web of interbreeding across Eurasia, suggests to us that modern humans did not have a single African origin, but rather emerged from a network of archaic populations across the Old World. This interpretation misreads evidence. Interbreeding did occur, although it was limited and one-sided. The Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA found in non-African populations amounts to only 1-6% of their genomes. The remainder is overwhelmingly derived from a single source population. Additionally, no trace of these archaic hominins exists in sub-Saharan African populations, who retain the oldest human lineages. The network model confirms that the African lineage was the dominant one, absorbing small contributions from archaic populations encountered during expansion. The multi-regional model proposed that after Homo erectus left Africa nearly 2 million years ago, populations in Africa, Europe, and Asia continued to evolve in parallel, with enough gene flow between them to keep them a single species. Modern humans, in this view, would have emerged simultaneously across the Old World from local archaic ancestors. For decades, this was the main competitor to Out of Africa. There was supporting evidence by some fossil evidence of regional anatomical continuity (such as the fact that certain facial features in East Asia seemed to persist from Homo erectus to modern populations). However, when ancient DNA became available, the multiregional model collapsed. Genetic data showed that all non-African populations carry a distinct genetic signature that emerged in Africa within the last 100,000 years and spread outward. There’s no continuity of lineages from pre-sapiens populations in Europe or Asia into modern humans. Only the small admixture noted prior. The scientific case for Out of Africa seems to be settled. But for those who ask why humans are so diverse, why this particular species expanded so rapidly, and what the meaning of our unity and diversity might be, science offers limited answers. The anthroposophic spiritual-scientific framework helps us to situate Out of Africa within a broader narrative, becoming material testament to spiritual reality.

Humans and Apes

Before continuing, it is important to note one thing. The animal kingdom (including the apes) did not arise through a linear evolution from lower to higher culminating in humans. Instead, animals represent forms separated from the human stream at various stages of Earth evolution. During the Lemurian and Atlantean Epochs, the human physical form remained relatively “plastic” and capable of being shaped by the will, while certain group souls took hold of physical bodies that had hardened prematurely, giving rise to the various animal species. Apes in particular are described as a branch that split off during the Lemurian epoch, when the human form was still soft. Their physical bodies became fixed earlier, while the human form retained its malleability, allowing it to later receive the fully incarnated human “I”. Thus, the anatomical similarities between apes and humans are not evidence of common descent in the Darwinian sense, it is a reflection of a shared origin in the plastic human form from which apes diverged. This has implications for how we interpret the fossil hominins such as australopithecines, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and so on. They represent the various side branches or transitional forms that either preceded the full descent of the human “I” into physical bodies, or were left behind as the human form continued to refine and harden. The fact that the first stone tools appear with australopithecines shows that even before the “I” fully incarnated, beings with a degree of intelligence and will were interacting with the physical world. Steiner would likely say that these beings were ensouled by group souls (or by individual souls not yet fully independent) who were preparing the physical “vehicle” for the eventual incarnation of the fully self-conscious “I”. When we come to Homo sapiens in the Quaternary period, we see the completion of the process. The physical body is fully hardened, and the human “I” can incarnate into it with full self-consciousness/self-awareness. The Out of Africa populations can be said to represent the stock in which this final hardening and spiritual integration took place.

Physical Consolidation

The three periods we shall concern ourselves with are the Lemurian, Atlantean, and our current post-Atlantean epochs. The Middle Lemurian period, correlating roughly to the Mesozoic period, was the beginning of physical incarnation. The human body was still soft and malleable, bones were not fully mineralized, and so durable fossils did not exist. This was also following the time the sexed distinction made its emergence. The Atlantean period, correlating roughly to the Tertiary (the Paleogene and Neogene), found a progressive hardening of the world and of man. Bodies were much more liquid-like compared to today, yet the skeletal system was still not fully solid. The first durable artifacts of man appear only at the very end. It is with the Post-Atlantean period, correlating to the quaternary (Pleistoscene and Holocene), that “hardening” completed. Bones fully mineralized, the modern brain structure emerged (as evidenced by the Kalambo structure, demonstrating that even 500,000 years ago, beings on Earth were capable of planning and complex functions), and the human form as we know it today cemented itself. If Steiner’s account is accurate, we should expect the physical record to roughly correlate and show no durable evidence from the Lemurian epoch, because the body, and its lifestyle, did not leave much to be found. We should also find only sporadic, late evidence from the Atlantean epoch, as hardening gradually progressed (we will not touch the topic of inherited Atlantean wisdom in this article). Additionally, we should also find abundant evidence of fully modern humans from the post-Atlantean epoch onward. This is precisely what the fossil and archeological records reveal. During the Lemurian period, there are no accepted hominin fossils or stone tools. The few anomalous claims (such as the Eocene tools of the 19th century) have been refuted as either misinterpretations of the data or frauds. However, this does not serve as a problem, because it is exactly what one would expect if the human form has not yet reached a level of solidity capable of leaving durable remains (and based off the lifestyle of man during the Lemurian, they were much more nature attuned than we are today. The transformation/destruction of nature into tools would not occur until the Atlantean). For most of the period, there is no evidence of “man”. The first hominins appear only in the late Miocene and early Pliocene (Sahelanthropus, Orrorin, Ardipithecus), but they left no stone tools. Only at the very end of the Pliocene, around 3.3 million years ago, do we find the first undisputed stone tools at Lomekwi, Kenya. This is the point when Steiner would say hardening was approaching completion. As the Atlantean epoch gives way to the Quaternary, the first durable evidence of a being capable of thought and technology begins to appear. Finally, in the Quaternary period, with the beginning of the Pleistocene after the global flood, the picture begins to resemble what we are familiar with. We find the first fossils of Homo, the spread of stone tool industries across Africa and Eurasia, and eventually, around 300 thousand years ago, the appearance of the anatomically modern Homo sapiens. It is no coincidence that cognitive and technological sophistication became possible once the physical form had fully hardened. This, along with a myriad of other factors, such as the separation intwo sexes, made it possible for the kind of independent, discriminatory inner life we are familiar with today, including its offspring: technology.

The Out of Africa genetic evidence places the origin of all living Homo sapiens within the Post-Atlantean epoch. The mitochondrial and Y-chromosomal lineages that coalesce around 200-300 thousand years ago represent the survival of a particular population in Africa whose physical constitution had reached the modern level of mineralization. From this population all living humans have a connection. This does not mean that there were no humans before that time. It means that before the Post-Atlantean epoch, the human physical form had not yet achieved the degree of solidity that would allow it to fossilize in the kind of way we recognize today, and that the genetic lineages of those earlier forms either died out or were absorbed into the later, more hardened populations. In this way, the Out of Africa bottleneck is a genetic signature, representing the final stabilization of the modern human body.

Origin of the Races

If the human physical form only fully hardened in the Quaternary, then the diversity we see among human populations today must have arisen after that consolidation. For those that have followed thus far, this conclusion certainly makes sense. It is also consistent with the scientific view that the major physical differences among human groups are recent adaptations to different climates and environments, occurring within the last 100 thousand years or so. Steiner provides us with another, higher, aspect. The diversity we see appears to us as a matter of natural selection, or this or that theory, acting on a uniform “stock”. It may do something to that effect, but it primarily reflects the lingering influence of the spiritual forces that shaped the human body during the long epochs of hardening. The different “racial” characteristics are expressions of the different planetary and Luciferic influences that worked on the human form during the Lemurian and Atlantean epochs, only becoming fixed in the Post-Atlantean period.

In the middle of the Lemurian epoch, when the human physical form became capable of hosting the self-conscious “I”, souls began to incarnate from different planetary spheres (the beginning of physical existence, and the sexed distinction, was also the beginning of reincarnation!). This includes beings not only from Earth’s sphere, but also from Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, and other regions. These souls brought with them different spiritual-soul qualities, inclinations, temperaments, and karmic backgrounds that suited them for particular forms of earthly experience. The souls that descended from Saturn, for instance, had a natural affinity for a more inward, contemplative life; those that came from Mars brought a more active, will-oriented nature; those from Jupiter carried a certain wisdom and sense of the cosmic order. At that time, the human physical body was relatively malleable. The group souls that guided these incoming spirits worked from within to shape the body in accordance with the spiritual predisposition of each group. This is why, already in the Lemurian epoch, distinct “types” of humanity began to emerge. They would be tendencies that would later solidify into “races”.

An important factor in the formation of the races was the influence of the Luciferic beings. Luciferic spirits (particularly the aberrant Spirits of Form, also called the Luciferic Spirits of Motion) intervened in the evolution of humanity during the Lemurian and Atlantean epochs. Their goal was to accelerate the independence of the human “I” at the cost of disrupting the original unity of the human physical and etheric constitution. Had these beings not intervened, humanity would have developed from a single, uniform physical type that would have changed only slowly and in harmony with the Earth’s transformation. Instead, the Luciferic forces introduced differentiation. They hardened certain groups more quickly, fixed certain physical traits, and caused the stock of man to split into varied forms that adapted to different geographic and spiritual conditions. Steiner describes the abnormal Spirits of Form, working from the astral, taking hold of the etheric and physical bodies of different groups, imprinting them with characteristics that would later become hereditary. It’s important to clarify that these beings are not evil in the moral sense, but that their activity created the conditions for human diversity. While these also brought along with it the potential for egoism and separation, it also brought the need for the unifying force of the Christ impulse.

By the Atlantean epoch, the differentiation had crystallized into seven sub-races, from which the main racial types of the current Post-Atlantean epoch descended. At the end of Atlantis, when the continent was destroyed by the great flood (associated with the ice ages at the boundary between the Tertiary and Quaternary periods), populations migrated in different directions. According to Steiner, five main racial remnants emerged from this dispersal. I must mention importantly that these races are not to be confused with the modern day racial categories.

The first is the Black race, These are descendants of the earliest Atlantean sub-races, carrying the oldest physical forms. Their constitution is characterized by a strong astral body, making them receptive to spiritual forces in a more instinctive, dreamlike way. The next is the Yellow race, originating from the later Atlantean sub-races that migrated eastward into Asia. Their etheric body is particularly strong, giving them a higher rapport with the vital forces of nature, expressed in their languages and cultural forms. The next is the Red “American Indian” race, a branch that migrated westward into the Americas. They retained certain archaic qualities, including what Steiner calls a “memory-based consciousness”, and a close relation to the land. This red race of ancient times are not to be confused with the Native Americans of today, they represent a “declining” branch of humanity. More to be said on this later. The penultimate race is the Brown race, a mixed group which spread across the islands of Southeast Asia and the Pacific. Their physical form shows a blend of influences, and their constitution tends towards a certain dreaminess and passivity. Finally is the White race, descended from the fifth Atlantean sub-race (known as the “primal Semites”), which had its main center in what is now Ireland. This group migrated eastward across Europe and Asia, becoming the bearers of logical consciousness, the faculty that would come to full development in the Post-Atlantean epoch. Such migrations established the broad racial categories that have persisted into historical times. However, it is important to understand that the “races” we observe today are not identical to the original Atlantean forms. Over millennia, the intermixing with Homo sapiens and various other hominids, adaptation to new environments, and the ongoing process of soul incarnation have continuously reshaped these populations. The categories and characteristics that seem to hold so much weight today are in fact an outer residue of a process which began in the Lemurian period and is now gradually coming to the end.

As regarding the modern “races”, these five are definitely not “pure” types; they have intermingled throughout history well into our current Post-Atlantean period. The boundaries are fluid, and the characteristics attributed to the archaic races are general tendencies, not static absolutes. Migration and population is a very complex thing, involving moving over time, backflows of migration (creating a recursive situation), and internal differentiation within a given population. Throughout time, a tendency towards the flourishing of the individual has emerged, helped by the fact that Steiner emphasizes that these racial differences are not permanent. In the future, as humanity evolves toward spiritual unity, the concept of race will lose its meaning. For more on this, I have written two articles: The Problem of Africa in German Idealism and Anthroposophy’s Treatment as a Response to Afro-Pessimism and Post-Scripts to the Racialism in Anthroposophy

Each of the five main races is associated with a specific geographical region from which certain cosmic forces ebb and flow. Steiner indicates that there are five centers on Earth - in Africa, Asia, the Americas, and elsewhere - where the etheric forces that shape racial characteristics are concentrated. These centers are points through which planetary influences (the same planets that hosted the descending souls) work on the human constitution. The Lemurian and Atlantean epochs were the periods of race formation, a time when the physical and etheric bodies of man were molded into distinct types under the influence of spiritual beings. The Post-Atlantean epoch, by contrast, is the period of soul development. The task for this epoch is not to create new races (although one trip to New York and you will find combinations never before conceived, Yemeni-Puerto Ricans and Bengali-Russians alike). It is a time for the cultivation of the individual “I”, enabling man to overcome the limitations of heredity and race. This is why, in the Post-Atlantean epoch, the concept of race loses its significance. The five main races, therefore, are remnants of an earlier stage of evolution. Their continued existence serves a purpose only insofar as they provide a variety of physical and etheric conditions through which the soul can gain a kind of training, but the direction of evolution is towards unity. Like a teacher who has finished teaching a student erases their vocation, leaving behind a learned pupil, so too shall the racial conditions serve their purpose for the development of man.

Adam and Eve Revisited: One and Too Many

What is left after the initially unified “man” having been a purely spiritual form, then concretized and materialized into the many kinds of man, is revealed to be a transitionary stage towards a reunion of unity? We may bring to the fore the archetypes which underlie the unity of man: Adam and Eve. It is quite fitting that modern science has taken the symbolic archetypes found in the bible in its monikers of “Y-chromosomal Adam” and “Mitochondrial Eve”. The familiar biblical account of Adam and Eve has long been read as the story of two individual human beings, the first man and woman from whom all humanity descended. Modern genetics has shown that this literal interpretation cannot stand. There was no “first man” or “first woman” in the sense of a solitary breeding pair. Yet the truth of the narrative, properly understood illuminates the findings of the mineralistic sciences. We will re-appropriate these figures in order to help us understand them as the primal polarity of group spirit and group soul, and check the unending modulation of the one and many.

The two figures are not physical individuals. They represent the group spirit/soul of nascent humanity. Adam (“the one taken from the earth”) stands for the unified “I-being” of humanity, or in other words, the collective spiritual identity from which all individual human spirits descend. Eve (“the animate one”, or “the mother of all living”) represents the group soul, the diversifying principle that gives form to the physical, etheric, and astral bodies of human beings across the Earth. Spiritually, we are one. All human souls share the same origin in the Adam-I. Physically, we are many. The Eve-soul differentiates itself into the varied forms and temperaments that allow souls to undergo the experience and training necessary for their evolution.

The more esoteric connection between the names Yahweh and Eve goes even further. Yahweh, the “Earth Lord”, is the being who guides the development of the human “I” on Earth. The name Yahweh contains the Yodh, which represents the individual I-being, that glimmer of selfhood that descends into the human soul. Eve, as the “Earth Mother” embodies the accumulated forces of the Old Saturn, Old Sun, and Old Moon stages of Earth evolution, forces which build the physical, etheric, and astral bodies that serve as a dwelling place for the “I”. Thus in the name “Yahweh”, we have the union of the spiritual I (the Adam principle), with it’s concretized form (the Eve principle). This union is the true “first couple”, the foundational polarity of spirit and soul that makes human existence on Earth possible. With this in mind, the apparent contradiction between man’s genetic unity and physical diversity dissolves. The one is the Adam-I, the unified spiritual origin of all human beings. Every human soul, regardless of race or epoch, participates in the same I-being. This is partly why, despite all the outward differences, we can recognize one another as fellow humans, why the deepest spiritual development always leads to the experience of a universal brotherhood. The differences end up negligible. The “many” is the Eve-soul, the variable manifest as the variety of physical forms, temperaments, and cultural expressions. The Eve-soul is a collective soul that “gives birth” to the multiplicity of human forms. Hence, from a single spiritual stock, diversity in man can come about.

Through the development of the “I”, and reception of the Christ impulse, individuals can increasingly free themselves from the constraints of heredity and race. The Pauline saying, that “There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus” makes this goal express. The reunification of the many into one, without the loss of individuality, is a foregone conclusion.

item.title: Experience Fetishism
item.description:
It’s often that one hears from the hylics, a call to expand the bank of potential experiences to draw from in some effort to be open-minded. The pressure is on to have “authentic” travel epiphanies or try new foods, and submerse oneself in a foreign-yet-familiar environment. How can it be the case that while the world (particularly the Western developed industrial centers) urges everyone to experience more, we are left feeling emptier and performative in our lives? This failing is certainly not for lack of experiences, or having too few experiences. It results from a confusion of two different kinds of interfacing. Our social machinery is designed to sell us a superficial experience while depriving us of direct engagement, only leaving an implicit access point for genuine connection for those who have been enlightened due to a disciplined study, or enlightenment on the topic by way of desperation; a necessity in the face of the superficiality of #mindfulness retreats. For the hylics who claim that this or that experience is a must, for the social media posting of travel around the world to custom tailored tourist destinations, and to the ones thinking them have escaped this superficiality only to unrealize that the superficiality is something intrinsic and not to be found in the object of experience, Seneca the Younger’s observation that ‘we are as much afraid in the light as children in the dark’ serves as reminder to the fact of paralysis. Such a paralysis indicates a new kind of fear to be found in the fear of meaninglessness hidden beneath the gallery of endless consumable experiences.

Such a confusion, in addition to in part being a personal failing due to lack of reflection or engagement, is conditioned by a system which is conducive to vapid testimony. This system has been previously diagnosed by thinkers like Walter Benjamin in his text The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction. Benjamin observed that mechanical reproduction, which can be defined here as the ability to mass produce perfect copies of a work of art, strips art of its “aura”. An artworks’ aura is its unique presence spatiotemporally, it’s particular novelty in the world. This loss parallels the replacement of a true experience - something that is contextual and in some cases challenging - with a superficial, reproducible engagement. Take, for example, the Mona Lisa. The “true” experience might involve understanding its Renaissance context and subsequently having a personal, contemplative moment before it. The modern, commodified engagement is often a struggle through a crowd to snap a picture of a surprisingly small painting, a photo which is identical to millions of others, reducing the encounter to a reproducible visual token. Not too unlike the trend of abstraction in various aspects of modern engagement with the world. Here, there is a direct loss of “aura” in favor of a consumable, shareable moment. Theodor Adorno and Max Horkheimer coined the term “culture industry” to describe how capitalism transforms art and ideas into standardized, predictable commodities designed for passive consumption, not active thought or genuine emotion. Today, this analysis finds its ultimate expression in the “Experience Economy”. The culture industry no longer sells us films or music as it did in the past. Instead, it sells us pre-packaged transformations and unreal enlightenment, which may take the form of films and music, but certainly belittle the very medium of their conveyance.

The curated mindfulness retreat, trips to Bali or the slave state of Dubai, the “must-do” travel itinerary around the world (which is usually just Europe, America, and Japan, with exceptions made for those in the orbit of Western polarity), even the formulaic self-help bestseller are all products of this extended culture industry, and what I term “experience fetishism”. They take the deeply personal human desires of growth and connection and repackage it in standardized, sterilized, purchasable products, in effect, making this superficiality the default mode of engagement. People start to crave this superficiality in an effort to feed the gnawing void left by the very introduction of this superficiality. This system does more than sell us things, it sells us pre-fabricated versions of our own inner lives, pacifying our longing for depth by offering a convincing (to the hylic) simulation of it. That fear which Seneca spoke of stems from the consumption of these simulations, while our innate need goes unmet. What is described here is a system of displacement. We begin to confuse the marketed simulation of depth for depth itself. Intellectualism transforms into the accumulated experiential objects which mind turns over from one, to the next, indifferently. The defenders of the West on Twitter (which often times are not from the West) have committed the very shameful act of turning their very own history into an amusement park of modern signifiers and ideas about the past. In turn, the defense of such a project is as immaterial as defending one’s own physical body while being immersed in open waters. If not by the forces of nature and metabolism inevitably wearing this defense down, any natural contingency such as a shark or school of venomous jellyfish may decide to initiate its doom, whichever way the wind blows.

We collect signifiers of a meaningful life, whether it is photos or branded merchandise of various “journeys”, all while finding the substance elusive. We become, as Benjamin might have feared, collectors of copies in a world that has made the original encounter seem either inefficient or intimidating. It is not only the “Funko Pop” or “Labubu” collectors that are worthy of derision. Experience fetishism is thus the compulsive pursuit of curated, consumable sensations as a substitute for (and often an obstacle to) meaningful and transformative engagement with the world. It is the reduction of the world to a gallery of commodifiable moments and objects, where the primary value of an activity is its ability to be captured and catalogued as a personal asset, often times to auction in a system and society of spectacle, rather than its capacity to fundamentally alter one’s understanding or character. It transforms the human pursuits of travel, leaning, spirituality, and relationships, pale imitations of what they are, and alienates the objects of experience themselves from their own inner processes and concept. There is a valuing of the immediate thrill and peripheral novelty over the gratifying work of reflection and integration into wisdom. This worth is judged by its social currency, the power to generate envy or admiration, and, in most cases, validation from an external audience. This generates a checklist mentality towards life where one must see this, visit that, or try this, driven by social pressure and FOMO instead of curiosity or personal calling. Experience fetish creates a “cult of the must”.

The fetish is enchanting precisely because it supposedly offers a beatific simplification. It promises the delicacies of life while bypassing the vulnerability, work, and discipline required for these things. Where it casts a spell where the signifier shines so brightly that it obscures the active erasure of the signified, the dispelling is found in the inversion of the result. One must begin with dismantling the enchanted object and embracing the disenchanting work of genuine engagement. Once this is done, a realization occurs where the enchanted object is blindingly apparent that it has been enchanted by Mammon, and this disenchanting effort of inversion unveils itself to be the necessary step in true comprehension and experience. By taking this act, one goes from having an experience, to allowing an experience to have you, and being permanently marked by it.

item.title: Idle Talk in the Age of TikTok: On Linguistic Decay
item.description:
Of all the human faculties, language is perhaps the most intimate and mysterious, being responsible for conjuring emotions in the soul, and drawing out latent potential, all by an invocation of symbols and signs that we use daily. In its original and true nature, language was Logos, the divine creative principle that ordered the cosmos. The Word was understood in the past as an active power, a vibration that participated directly in the essence of what it named. There existed no arbitrary divide between the signifier and the signified, sound and being been fused. To utter a true name was therefore not to label a thing from the outside, but to invoke its reality and align with its spiritual core. This ontological unity rendered language inherently sacramental. Blessings, curses, and ritual incantations were seen as having immediate metaphysical effects because speech was an act of cosmic participation, distinct from its conception today as mere formality or social convention. This sanctity gave rise to the concept of holy languages. Traditions revered Sanskrit, Hebrew, Examination Chinese, Quranic Arabic, Biblical Latin, and so on and so forth, as Marshall McLuhan rightfully commented, as revealed architectures, or novel vessels for truth. To think, pray, or compose in these languages was to operate within a (and the) divine framework, the only medium capable of housing certain transcendental realities. Furthermore, this unity was experienced sensibly. The resonance of a word was organically tied to its meaning. Phenomena like the Bouba/Kiki effect (where people intuitively associate rounded sounds with rounded shapes and vice versa) are thus interpreted as the surviving remnants of this primordial unity. They are fleeting proof that the human soul retains a memory of a time when form and essence, or sound and sense, were one.

An ancient view this is, if for the fact that it casts our contemporary experience of language into a puzzling relief. For, we do not live in a world of true names and efficacious words. We inhabit a universe of arbitrary signs, where cat could just as easily be “gato”, “neko”, or “Katze”, as a matter of social agreement and contingency (or in some cases a view which deprives language of spiritual significance but has to concede that it fits mechanically in one way or another) rather than an explicit connection with cosmic truth. There is not a discrepancy, this in fact evidences a great decay from language as a participatory event, into convention. The fall from logos is the story of language becoming unmoored from being, a process intrinsically linked to the evolution of consciousness itself. This shift is a necessary, if not arduous, stage in Spirit’s journey. It corresponds to the epochal awakening of the Consciousness Soul - the modern principle of self-reflective individualism. To achieve self-awareness and freedom, human consciousness had to turn decisively inward, withdrawing its projective participation from the world. This inward turn, mirroring modernity’s deterritorializing force, required the very severing of the ancient bond between word and thing. Language had to be drained of its innate albeit given meaning, so that the sovereign “I” could become capable of grasping meaning from within as well as without. The celebrated arbitrariness of the sign, therefore, is in a way a diagnostic mark of this age. What linguistics posits as a neutral principle is the very mechanism of alienation required for the birth of the modern ego. The word becomes an empty shell, a detached signifier, because the conscious self needed a neutral tool to wield, rather than a sacred power to which it was subject.

The historical indices of this fall are certainly tangible. The evolution from pictographic to phonetic writing is a tell-tale sign. Hieroglyphs and ideograms maintained an iconic, mimetic link to the world. For example, a picture of a sun still carried a trace of the sun’s essence. The alphabet, a triumph of abstraction, reduces speech to a code of neutral symbols representing pure sound. This form of language is the perfect technology (in the sense of techne) for the Consciousness Soul, seeing as it is analytic efficient, and severed from iconic presence. In addition to this, it enables the dissemination of abstract thought, but at the same time finalizes the divorce of the written word from the living cosmos. This abstraction was compounded by the “scientific” and nominalist worldviews, which exiled qualities and essences from the material world, leaving only quantities and names. Language, in this world, can only point to surfaces, and likewise the commonality which etymology sheds light on can only reasonably signify a correlation of concept with the convenience of spatiotemporal form. Furthermore, the Bouba/Kiki effect now appears in a tragic light as a testament to what was lost. It tells of old modes of consciousness and participatory magic (and language, back then, was by all means magic) flaring up in a mind otherwise conditioned to see sound and shape as separate domains. It is the soul’s muted protest against its own conceptual imprisonment.

Thus, the modern condition of language as a formal system, as a convenient but hollow geometry for the ego’s use, is the subjective reality of this objective historical stage. The feeling that language is an internal combinatory space is not its eternal truth, it is only what is left after the spirit of Logos has withdrawn. However, it is not all dire. The great emptying of meaning in the world, can also be considered a great preparation. It is the necessary negative moment in the process of totality. By passing through this desert of abstraction, by experiencing language as a detached and formal medium, the Consciousness Soul gains the unprecedented capacity for critical self-reflection and free conceptual recognition. It must lose the given, participatory Word, to eventually find it again, consciously and from within. The arbitrary language of the modern age is therefore the condition in which the possibility of a future redeemed speech is created. Having fully integrated the power of abstract thought, humanity will one day speak with the authority of Logos.

For now, we have to deal with what Heidegger called “Idle Talk”. The ultimate manifestation of this emptied, circulating language, is what Heidegger characterizes as speech that has completely lost its anchor in the primordial, disclosive power of Logos. It is language reduced to a self-referential social mechanism. Words are passed along not to reveal the truth of a matter, but precisely to avoid encountering it. In Idle Talk, understanding/substance (they are etymologically the same word, fun fact!) is simulated through the effortless absorption of pre-digested and “average” meanings. The reality of the arbitrary sign is a closed circuit of chatter where words refer only to other words, creating a comforting shallow consensus that shields the modern ego from the demanding world-disclosing responsibility of authentic speech. This is the sacred Word that once invoked being, having devolved, in its most common social form, into a background noise of cliché and hearsay- it is the sound of consciousness speaking to itself from within itself.

A descent into abstraction needs a habitat, and it finds this habitat in the digital realm of the internet, the perfect expression of modernity’s de-temporalized, deterritorialized space. Here, the inward-turning Consciousness Soul constructs a world in its own image featuring a realm of pure information where language exists in a state of frictionless circulation, liberated not only from the object, but from the being and body of the Earth. The internet is the ecosystem in which idle talk becomes the dominant, self-replicating mode of being, setting the state for a platform like TikTok to emerge as the apotheosis of post-literate communication.

The linguistic ecology of TikTok represents the logical result of this trajectory in the form of the metastasis of Idle Talk into an optimized and fully realized system. The platform’s hallmarks of gratuitous abbreviation, intentional misspellings, syntactic compression, and the rapid, vampiric repurposing of words (particularly from African American Vernacular English) into mimetic signals are the signature of language accelerating into pure social functionality, shedding any remaining pretensions to depth, history, or disclosure. When “because” becomes “bc”, or “this shit” becomes “ts”, or complex (in form, not in substance) statements are cramped into acronyms, we witness the final abstraction of the sign. The word lacks being even a full shell- it is fragment. It is a token whose value is determined solely by its velocity and adhesive power within the circuit of communal recognition.

This is Idle Talk weaponized and accelerated by the habitat of the internet. Heidegger’s concept feared a chatter that levels all understanding to the average, whereas TikTok’s architecture actively enforces it. Algorithmic virality rewards surface level novelty and in-group fluency, incentivizing the continuous production of linguistic shortcuts that bypass conscious reflection by the subject, in order to trigger instant affective or tribal response. The intentional “wrongness” of a spelling is a performative rejection of external authority, creating a closed vernacular that fortifies community boundaries on the one hand, and further divorces the sign from any stable referent on the other. “Rizz” and “sigma” are not in a dictionary (yet, as far as I know), but still exists in the collective performance of its use as a purely circulating social value.

In a sense, we can call this beyond illiteracy (although America has a major illiteracy problem, and you’d expect nothing less from a democratic nation), into post-literacy, defined as the linguistic state perfectly adapted to the Consciousness Soul’s internalization. Language has become a pure tool of the sovereign ego, constructing transient identities and affiliations in a digital das Man. However, even within this schema, even in the apparent decay, a purpose is served. TikTok language, as the extreme of Idle Talk, makes the alienation of the sign total and unmistakable. By reducing communication to its most fluid and disposable form, it forces a kind of crisis. One either surrenders to endless circulation for its own sake, or begins to feel the hunger for a Word that means. In this sense, the vernacular of the platform serves as a negative positivity. It is the resonant sound of the hollow Logos, waiting to be filed again by a spirit that has fully passed through the task of its own grasping, waiting to be recognized from within and from without.

item.title: Libya under Gaddafi: The Only African Country in North Africa?
item.description:
To be or not to be (African)?

The recent AFCON 2025 win by Senegal (versus Morocco) has brought to mind an inflammatory question, or rather an observation, which casts doubt on the nature of “North Africa” in the context of being a part of Africa. North Africa’s identity is the site of a fundamental antagonism wherein objectively it is an inescapable part of the African continent by every material measure (such as geologically it is located on the continent, and it’s history, which includes its political economy and historical trade routes and such). At the same time subjectively, its people’s self-determined identity is overwhelmingly anchored in the MENA (Middle East and North Africa) civilizational sphere, often defined in explicit contrast to “Africa”. This contradiction doesn’t strike me as a mistake to be solved, but the central dogma of the region’s modern condition. The very passion by which it disidentifies itself is proof of an unsettled relationship, rather than lack thereof. I am speaking, of course, of the anti-Blackness present in North Africa.

Ask a Moroccan or an Algerian if they are “African”, and see the varied response one will be met with. The answer, when and if it comes, is rarely a simple yes, barre the cases when conceding the very attachment of geography isn’t the framing of the question (so, answers like “of course it’s African, it is in Africa!”). “African”, if used at all, is often appended as a geographical afterthought- “we are in Africa, but we are not of it”. This is the crux of what can be called a painful schism. On the one side lies the objective and unchangeable fact of the being. On the other, lies the subjective force of self-conception which aligns itself historically, culturally, and religiously, with the Middle East and the Mediterranean; an alignment with the Arab-Islamic world of the MENA region. In a way, North Africa inherits Europe’s defining mode of metaphysics and interface with the cosmos as “a forgetting of Being”, while at the same time being rooted differently, or at least partially conditioned by, Africa. Clearly, this goes beyond syntactics and into semantics. To dismiss it with the pussyfooted stance some may call diplomatic, that North Africa is simply “both” is a cheap cop-out. It throws dirt on the actual self-identity of millions for whom “African” is not a badge of belonging, but an indication of a history where distance was constructed through centuries of civilization and trade.

Before we can ask if North Africa is a part of Africa, we must first as: What do we even mean by Africa? The continent’s very name is an external imposition. To the ancient Greeks and Romans, the land south of the Mediterranean was not “Africa” but Libya, a coastal strip distinct from the interior. The term Africa likely stems from the Roman designation “Africa Proconsularis” for their province around Carthage, and was gradually expanded by European cartographers and colonizers. By the 19th century Scramble for Africa, it solidified as the label for the continent. In this sense, it’s very self-conception is wrought with externality. However, the case can be made that the imposed frame was reclaimed from within via the anti-colonial and Pan-African movements of the 20th century, led by Ghana’s Kwame Nkrumah, and Tanzania’s Julius Nyerere as a rallying cry for solidarity and shared destiny. This was further institutionalized through the founding of the Organization of African Unity. Here, “Africa” has become an idea of unity against oppression (but still externally determined). Despite this, in popular vernacular a kind of reductionism persists. Globally, and importantly in some cases within North Africa itself, the term “African” has been racially narrowed to a metonym for sub-Saharan Africa. It carries a connotation of Blackness, and a perceived cultural sphere separate from the Mediterranean and Arab worlds. This reduction is a trap which instantly ensnares our initial question, making “Are you African?” feel, to many North Africans, like being asked “Are you Black, or are you from that other Africa?”. The question of “Africa’s” determination has yet to be proven, and has only been theorized as of thus far. The true test of it as a distinct being will only be proven in the actualized existence of its people. The more focused question one can ask then is “what is North Africa, such that it brings about distinction from Africa ‘proper’?”. To answer this question, it is necessary to point out a fundamental divide within the Arab-Islamic world itself: the Maghreb (الْمَغْرِب‎ - “the West,” encompassing Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, and often Mauritania) versus the Mashriq (الْمَشْرِق‎ - “the East,” the Arab states of the Middle East). A North African’s primary civilizational orientation is often first to this Maghreb-Mashriq continuum, not to a pan-African one.

Beneath this level lies the substance of the Amazigh, or Berber, people. These are the indigenous peoples of North Africa, with languages and cultures stretching back millennia before Phoenician or Arab arrival, and are the uncontested “African” layer of the region. To acknowledge the Amazigh is to acknowledge that North Africa’s deepest roots are autonomously and uniquely African, and not imported from the Middle East. This complicates any simple Arab vs Africa binary, and is key to understanding the regions’ character. Additionally, geography dictates an exception. The Nile Valley (primarily in Egypt and Sudan) forms a distinct corridor. Egypt’s history and psyche are fundamentally shaped by the river flowing from the African heartland. Sudan, even more explicitly, is a historical pivot point, a vast country torn by conflicts often framed as Arab versus African. This corridor ties North-Eastern Africa inextricably to the Sahel and the Horn, blurring any neat boundary and reminding us that the Sahara’s edges are zones of gradation and not distinct hard lined walls. Thus, we begin with contested definitions with “Africa” as an imposed label, a reclaimed project, and a racially narrowed colloquialism. “North Africa” is a modern geopolitical term masking a mixed reality of Arab-Islamic identity, Amazigh indigeneity, and the unifying exception of the Nile. It is within this tangled semantic field that the larger conflict plays out.

However fiercely identity may pull toward the Mediterranean and the Mashriq, North Africa remains wedded to the continent by chains of geography, history, and modern necessity. This is the objective, inarguable substrate upon which all subjective identities in the region have to contend with and integrate (sometimes unsuccessfully). North Africa rests upon the African tectonic plate; its mountains, faults, and mineral wealth are born of the same continental processes as the rest of Africa. The Sahara, so often imagined as a “barrier” is in fact a shared system which stretches from the Atlas foothills to the Sahelian shore. It is a connector that demands specialized adaptation, fostering trans-Saharan networks. Most definitively, the Nile River ( which we may call Egypt’s raison d’être) is an African river. Its lifeblood flows from the Ethiopian Highlands and the Great Lakes region. Egypt’s destiny is therefore irrevocably tied to the management, politics, and climate of the African interior. The historical tether cannot be ignored either, long predating the era of the Arab-Islamic. Ancient Egypt’s civilization cannot be understood outside its perpetual dialogue (whether through trade, warfare, and migration) with Nubia (aka modern Sudan), and cultures further South. The Kingdom of Kush conquered and ruled Egypt, a profound African reversal of power. Deeper in the desert, civilizations like the Garamantes (in modern Libya) built sophisticated subterranean irrigation systems and acted as crucial intermediaries, proving the Sahara was a space of commerce. Foundational to all is the Amazigh, the indigenous inhabitants whose presence for thousands of years constitutes the incontestable truth that the first “North Africans” were, and are, an African people.

The final link to “Africa proper” is the political-economic necessity of the 21st century. The various crises of the Sahel, where ever they come from (looking at you France), do not respect colonial borders, and spill northward. Meaning the instability in Mali or Niger becomes a direct security threat to Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya. Membership in the African Union also acts as a strategic platform for conflict mediation and collective bargaining. To abandon it would be to forfeit influence and abdicate responsibility for the systems which directly impact North African security and prosperity. North Africa is, in effect, bound by the objective fact sovereignty, which ties it to Africa. Thus, while a North African might look eastward toward Mecca in prayer, they stand atop African history and the African future of shared crises and fortunes. Any complete disavowal of “Africa” is a flight from the world. The land one stands on insists upon the very connection to Africa which some North Africans intend to disavow.

It’s strange, then, that the anchor is so very clearly African, but identity decisively points east. The allegiance of the region is certainly not to a continental project, it is towards the civilizational sphere of the MENA world as an active daily substance. It becomes less strange when one familiarizes themselves with a few “pillars” if you will. The first is the religion. Islam is the primary communal bond, and the foundational layer of social and spiritual life. Its culture and moral framework orient the believer towards the holy cities of Mecca and Medina in the Arabian Peninsula, and not towards the African interior. The Ummah is the imagined polity whose heartlands lie in Asia. The language goes hand in hand with Islam. Arabic is the language of the sacred text of the Quran, media, and politics. The lingua franca that ties a Moroccan student to a Lebanese novel, or an Algerian politician to a Qatari news (Al Jazeera comes to mind) is Islam. This shared linguistic worlds creates a continuity with the Mashriq, making Cairo and Beirut feel culturally closer than Bamako or Abuja. The daily cultural diet does not dissuade the continuity, either. The dominance of Arab satellite television (like the aforementioned Al Jazeera, MBC, etc.), and the circulation of Arab music, or Ramadan television dramas, creates a social world through the digital which faces East. One final factor to take into account is the historical context of Ottoman rule or influence, which brought centuries of integration into an Eastern empire, and the later anti-colonial struggle framed through the potent ideology of Pan-Arabism. Gamal Abdel Nasser or Ba’athist parties are dreams of Arab unity, not of African unity. The political imagination stands as horizontally Arab, not African.

Not African and not Arab? Not realistic.

This consolidation of MENA identity conditions an active dis-identification. “African”, often reductively coded as Black and sub-Saharan becomes the distinguishing other by which the social process that separates “us” from “them” takes place. This is heard in colloquial or casual speech and seen in social attitudes toward darker-skinned Africans. This attitude is reflected in media representations that subtly or overtly frame “Arab” North Africa as more civilized, developed, or historically significant than “Black” Africa. The distancing taking place here is hierarchical, and acts as a mechanism which maintains the coherence of the preferred self-conception as being solely in continuity with the Middle East, and coincidentally being placed in Africa. Thus, when asked about identity, the heart’s answer is drawn from this shared reality of politics, religion, culture, language, media, and so on and so forth, all flowing from the East. The African ground, for all its material weight, remains for many, a silent geographical contingency, overshadowed by the life of Arab-Islamic civilization. This is the powerful subjective truth that any honest analysis must confront.

With respect to this, we have a contradiction at the crux of North Africa’s identity. The vehement distancing from “Africa” is not evidence by a lack of relationship, it is the loudest pronouncement of its unresolved relationship with it. One does not build an identity against something that is irrelevant. The force of the rejection mirrors the depth of the historical “entanglement”, which is putting it lightly. The primary source of this ordeal is found in the Trans-Saharan Slave Trade. For over a thousand years, this civilizational-scale process crafted a brutal social world where the “African”, particularly the Black African from south of the desert, became synonymous with “other” and “servile” in North African societies. This process embedded a structural and psychological anti-Blackness into the fabric of the social relations, establishing a hierarchy of human value that placed the Arabized or Amazigh North African above the “abeed” (slave, a term still used derogatorily). This is the origin of the separation, a hierarchy native to the nation, but certainly reinforced by European colonialism. French and Italian administrators, armed with the pseudoscience of racial anthropology, pursued explicit “Mediterraneanist” or “Berberist” policies. They argued that North Africans (particularly Amazigh populations) were a white and noble race, distinct from and superior to the Negroes of sub-Saharan Africa. This attitude, being internalized by segments of the local elite, added a modern European-sanctioned racial taxonomy to the older Arabo-Islamic one. Being offered a bargain which North Africans could not refuse, they were to be placed on a higher rung of the colonial racial ladder, contingent on accepting their own differentiation from the content below. It is not a coincidence that the term here is bargain, harkening back to the Faustian Bargain, and the Heideggerian ‘forgetting of being” mentioned that North Africa has inherited from Europe. This is just one avenue to be explored.

In the modernist project of building independent nation-states, anti-Blackness became a functional “moment of differentiation”. For North African elites forging post-colonial identities, they required a “constitutive outside”, or an “other”, against which the modern national self could be defined. The constructed “Black African” serves this purpose perfectly. Defining the modern “Arab-Maghrebi” citizen involved a conscious (and in some cases unconscious) differentiation from this other, purifying the national-self image and aligning it with a perceived lineage of historic (Phoenician, Roman, Arab) Mediterranean civilization, in contrast to a stereotyped “primitive” and “tribal” Africa. We have now a contemporary performance of that centuries-old differentiation, a socio-political ritual that reinforces the borders of a non-African national identity. The scorn for the Black migrant is, tragically and ironically, a perverse reaffirmation of a separate self. North Africa’s “non-African” identity is, to a significant degree, built upon the history of its relationship with and by virtue of Africa.

To view this struggle as between Arab and African, is to miss the signals originating from North Africa itself which problematize this binary. We can look to the Amazigh Revival as an indigenous corrective. The fight for Tamazight language and culture is a reclamation of an African identity which predates Arabization by a thousand years. In this assertion, what is to be found is an assertion of North African identity that is uniquely African, yet distinct from Arab political hegemony and sub-Saharan cultures. Further south is the Sahara-Sahel zone, where one can interpret this land as a land of synthesis and differentiation- one which purist ideologies will be forced to categorize incorrectly, or count as a deviation from the norm of a worldview. Nations like Mali, Niger, and Sudan are crucibles where Arab and Amazigh North Africa has blended with Songhai, Fulani, and Nubian cultures for millennium. Timbuktu’s manuscripts, are written in Arabic script but preserve the knowledge of the Malian empire. This is a good example of “fusion”. These societies render the harsh distinction between northern littoral and southern forests moot.

Gaddafi

Gaddafi’s Libya stands apart. Isolated in the Arab world, he declared himself “an African, catalyzing a decade of objective institutional creativity aimed at nothing less than the political and economic unification of the African continent. His government became the primary architect and financier of the African Union’s transformative leap from the loose OAU to a more integrated body. Libya funded over 15% of the AU’s budget, championed the creation of the African Investment Bank and the African Monetary Fund, and was the driving force behind RASCOM, the first satellite for African telecommunications, breaking a foreign monopoly. He leveraged state wealth to build infrastructure across the continent and positioned Libya as the patron for liberation movements and marginalized regions. Gaddafi represented a tangible, institutional answer to the question of continental unity that far exceeded the symbolic gestures of other North African states. Unfortunately, this objective reality being created existed in a stark parallel to a subjective social reality it failed to fundamentally alter, or rather, did not get to alter. While the state built African unity, Libyan society, which carried the historical baggage of the trans-Saharan slave trade and hierarchical social structures, continued to exhibit widespread anti-Black prejudice This was visible in the treatment of sub-Saharan migrants. Gaddafi’s Pan-Africanism did not constitute a national reckoning with racism or stem from a grassroots embrace of African identity. This paradox (which is in reality, the paradox of North African identity) reached its climax after the 2011 NATO-led intervention. The instant evaporation of the Pan-African project revealed its precarious foundation- that it was an edifice built on state power and not on broad objective spiritual conviction. The subsequent descent of Libya into chaos including the resurgence of open slave markets targeting Black Africans, and the collapse of living standards (from one of Africa’s highest literacy rates and GDP per capita to a failed state) demonstrated that Gaddafi’s noble project of unification was doomed to fail if it did not reconstitute North African identity, or reconcile what it actually means to be a North African. The very fact that slavery re-emerged tells you something intrinsically about the social substance of the Libyan people, and by extension North Africans as a whole. That the post-Gaddafi landscape did not revert to a neutral “Libyan” identity, instead regressing into the raw, unmediated societal pathologies and tribal factions that the state had previously contained and channeled, illustrates that this was a reversion to an older, unhealed spirit. In other words, the reappearance of slavery is the return of the repressed historical spirit, proof that the social substance of the people had not been transformed. Most, if not all countries (barre Congo and Sudan), have confronted what it means to be an African (particularly this or that kind of African- Angolan, South African, Ethiopian, Kenyan, etc.), and so this brings a sense of closure to sub-Saharan countries that is felt in contrast when dealing with the North African countries. This is part of why Africans south of the Sahara may find it easier to forget North Africa, because they have not dealt with this intrinsic anti-Blackness. After all, besides Gaddafi’s attempt and the practical establishment of the OAN (of which Morocco left in 1984 as a protest for the OAN recognizing the SADR. It must also be stated that Western Sahara was not part of historic Morocco in any meaningful sovereign sense, and so leaving the OAN in 1984, then applying for membership in the EEC is not a good look), the only interface with North Africans that sub-Saharans have is from North Africans arguing why they should be able to say the n-word. There is no real nationally recognized effort in reconstituting any kind of North African identity.

Gaddafi’s project, therefore, was not wrong in its vision, but premature in its historical moment. It demonstrated that objective institutions cannot survive if they outpace the zeitgeist. One can say this was a failure of synchronization. The projects’ destruction demonstrates for us that the central unresolved problem for North Africa is existential. What does it mean to be North African? Until this question is answered in a way that reconciles body and spirit, heaven and earth, all grand political architectures in North Africa will remain vulnerable. The future of North Africa as an integral part of Africa does not hinge on more trade agreements or AU resolutions, though these are necessary. It hinges on an existential, spiritual reckoning that has yet to occur, one that is to be found in a collective and conscious confrontation with the historical that created its dis-identity. Until it does, North Africa will remain what it is today: the continent’s estranged shore, physically present but spiritually separated, building its identity upon the forgetting of its deepest and most intimate connection. The path belonging to North Africa is not eastward, it is inward, into a history it must remember down into the plains and forests across the desert, and inward into itself into a self it has yet to become.

item.title: Man, Animal, and Evolution: The Relationship Finally Clarified
item.description:
Intro

After writing my recent article Epistle to Polygenism, a few insights came to mind regarding the human form and it’s arrangement, going hand in hand with an elaboration of the relationship between man and animals. This article is on this consideration. In this undertaking, the process describing the generation of embodied form these beings take is necessary, namely, an explication of “evolution”, which is the name for this process. In its conventional usage, “evolution” refers generally to the gradual physical transformation of species over geological time, driven by variations and natural selection (which is a catch-all term employed to describe a failure for an organism to reach biotic potential). This framework has provided the world with a few insights, surely, yet it leaves more basic questions unanswered. Why did self-consciousness emerge only in one lineage? What is the inner relationship between the human form and the animal forms that so closely resemble it? Why does the fossil record show long periods of status punctuated by sudden appearances, rather than the smooth continuum the theory might predict?

To address these, we must expand our understanding of evolution to include dimensions that the physical sciences, by their very methodology, cannot access. Spiritual-scientific research offers precisely such an expansion. In this account, evolution is not merely the history of bodily forms. It involves the evolution of the planet, of spirits, or the ideas and concepts by which perceptions are generated by, the whole of nature, and so on and so forth. Thus far, the only theory in materialistic sciences approaching such a conception is the Expanding Earth hypothesis, which can explain everything modern plate tectonics does except for not taking as an assumption the fixed radius of the Earth over time.
